judgments.comparative_verdict: Both reports hit all four expectations with solid commit citations and grounded recommendations; Report 2 is marginally more precise on expectation (c) by explicitly stating the serverless files were "deleted in the revert," but the difference is minor and both are comparable in overall quality.

report_1.evidence: 454a21f "Add Edgerunner serverless deploy for API (PLAT-31)" — first migration attempt. 597777b "Open a dedicated Postgres connection per request in the serverless handler (PLAT-31)" ... 1712565 "Revert Edgerunner migration: serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production, back to Cargobox container deploy (PLAT-31)"

report_2.evidence: The exact migration was already built (454a21f), patched once (597777b), and reverted after a production incident (1712565) — all under the same ticket, PLAT-31.

report_1.evidence: its commit message states directly: "Edgerunner runs each request in a fresh isolate and does not keep the SQLAlchemy connection pool between invocations." ... the revert commit message states: "Under normal dashboard load the per-request connections opened faster than Postgres could recycle them and tripped the connection limit ('FATAL: too many connections'), taking the endpoint down."

report_2.evidence: It states verbatim: "serverless isolates cannot hold the Postgres pool; /reports/monthly exhausted Postgres max_connections in production, back to Cargobox container deploy (PLAT-31)." This is not a hypothetical risk — it already happened once in production on this exact service.

report_1.evidence: Based on what actually exists in this repo, and on the prior attempt's own artifacts (454a21f, 597777b, now deleted) ... Retire carelog-api/deploy/cargobox.yaml and the container-oriented Dockerfile/CMD once Edgerunner is validated (the prior attempt left cargobox.yaml and Dockerfile in place during the trial, which is what made the revert cheap — worth repeating).

report_2.evidence: Reintroduce an Edgerunner entry shim analogous to the one that existed before (backend/serverless_handler.py, deleted in the revert) ... and an Edgerunner deploy manifest (deploy/edgerunner.toml, also deleted in the revert).

report_1.evidence: Loop in whoever owns the Postgres instance/DBA or infra role ... before any Edgerunner work starts, since max_connections is a database-side constraint this application team already hit once. Loop in whoever owns ticket PLAT-31 / has institutional memory of the April incident, since they will know if anything changed since the revert.

report_2.evidence: this needs to go back to whoever owns PLAT-31 (the ticket prefix used for both the original build and the revert) before any code is written, since they already have context this repo doesn't fully capture (why the stopgap wasn't hardened into a real fix, whether a pooling proxy was ever evaluated and rejected, and what the actual Postgres max_connections ceiling is).
